title: Fight corruption and recover stolen funds
promise: Promised an all-out war on corruption, recovery of looted funds, and prosecution of those who stole public money.
assessment: High-profile EFCC prosecutions and asset recovery — including the Abacha loot repatriation deals — were notable. However, critics noted selective prosecution and continued immunity for political allies. OECD estimates roughly $1 billion was recovered during the term.

title: Revive the economy and diversify away from oil
promise: Promised to diversify Nigeria's oil-dependent economy, grow agriculture, and achieve economic self-sufficiency.
assessment: Nigeria experienced two recessions under Buhari (2016 and 2020). Agriculture sector grew, partly via the Anchor Borrowers' Programme. Oil still accounted for over 80% of foreign exchange earnings at end of term. GDP per capita fell in dollar terms.

title: Defeat Boko Haram and restore security in the North-East
promise: Declared Boko Haram technically defeated in 2015 shortly after taking office. Promised to rescue all Chibok girls and restore normalcy to the North-East.
assessment: Boko Haram lost significant territory and fractured into splinter groups. 107 of the 276 Chibok girls were rescued or freed. However, insurgency continued in a degraded form, and banditry expanded to the North-West. Full normalcy was not achieved.

title: Fix infrastructure — roads, rail, and power
promise: Promised to invest in rail rehabilitation, fix major highways, and dramatically increase electricity supply.
assessment: Major rail projects completed: Lagos-Ibadan rail, Abuja-Kaduna line extended, Itakpe-Warri line. Road rehabilitation was uneven. Power generation capacity increased modestly but outages remained chronic; average grid output stayed below 5,000MW against a demand of 30,000MW+.

title: Create jobs and reduce unemployment
promise: Pledged mass job creation through industrialisation, agriculture, and small business support.
assessment: Official unemployment rose from roughly 10% in 2015 to 33% by Q4 2020 (NBS methodology). Youth unemployment exceeded 42%. The N-Power programme enrolled about 500,000 graduates in temporary public work, falling far short of demand.
